concise:
Ayah 1–22 · linked video: 01. Qaf (Ayah 1-22)

Surah Qaf: Commentary on Ayahs 1-22

Ayah 1

قٓ ۚ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ٱلْمَجِيدِ

Qaf. By the glorious Quran.

Surah Qaf is the 50th surah of the Quran, revealed during the early Makkan period—specifically within the first four years after the beginning of revelation. Like many surahs from this era, it features shorter ayahs and rapid syllables that create a powerful rhythmic impact.

The surah opens with the mysterious letter Qaf, one of the disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة) that begin certain surahs. Immediately following this, Allah ﷻ takes an oath by the Quran itself, describing it as (المجيد)—the Majestic, the Glorious, the Noble.

The word Majd in Arabic refers to something that commands respect and inspires awe. It describes grandeur that cannot be ignored—something before which people naturally feel reverence. When Allah ﷻ swears by the Quran and describes it as Majid, He establishes from the very first ayah that this Book deserves to be held in the highest esteem. It is not merely words on pages, but a revelation filled with majesty and glory that should humble anyone who encounters it.

Ayah 2

بَلْ عَجِبُوٓا۟ أَن جَآءَهُمْ مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا شَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ

But they wonder that there has come to them a warner from among themselves, and the disbelievers say, “This is an amazing thing.”

Despite the glorious nature of the Quran, the immediate response from the disbelievers of Makkah was not acceptance but bewilderment. Allah ﷻ describes their reaction: they found it strange that a warner came to them from among their own people. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was not a foreigner or stranger—he was one of them, from their own tribe, speaking their own language. Yet this familiarity itself became a source of their rejection.

The disbelievers called this “a strange thing” (شَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ). What they found most difficult to accept wasn’t actually the message itself at this stage—it was the entire concept of revelation, prophethood, and accountability in the afterlife. The early Makkan message focused heavily on establishing these fundamental truths: that there is a Day of Judgment, that human beings will be held accountable, and that this life is not the end.

Ayah 3

أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا ۖ ذَٰلِكَ رَجْعٌۢ بَعِيدٌ

When we have died and become dust, [return is] that a far-fetched return?"

Here we see the core of their disbelief articulated clearly. The Quraysh couldn’t wrap their minds around resurrection. Their argument was simple: “When we die and our bodies turn to dust—when we completely disintegrate and become soil—how could we possibly return to life? That’s a farfetched return (رَجْعٌۢ بَعِيدٌ).”

The word (بَعِيدٌ) means distant or farfetched. They considered resurrection not just unlikely, but impossibly remote. Once the body has decomposed, once it has been scattered as dust, how could those particles be reassembled into a living, breathing human being? This seemed beyond the realm of possibility to them.

Ayah 4

قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا تَنقُصُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَعِندَنَا كِتَـٰبٌ حَفِيظٌۢ

We know what the earth takes away from them, and with Us is a recording book.

Allah’s ﷻ response to their doubts is direct and powerful. He declares: We already know exactly what the earth takes away from them. As bodies decompose in the grave—as the earth slowly consumes them, particle by particle, as worms and natural processes break down the flesh—Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge of every atom, every molecule, every fragment.

The phrase “We know what the earth takes away” (مَا تَنقُصُ ٱلْأَرْضُ) uses the verb tanqusu, which means to decrease or diminish gradually. Allah ﷻ is aware of this gradual process of decay in minute detail.

Furthermore, Allah ﷻ possesses a (كِتَـٰبٌ حَفِيظٌۢ)—a guarding book, a book that itself guards and preserves. This can be understood on two levels: First, it guards the record of every person’s deeds. The body may perish, but the soul continues, and the record of that person’s life is meticulously preserved. Second, this book guards the knowledge of every particle of every human being, making resurrection not just possible but certain. Nothing is lost to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 5

بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِىٓ أَمْرٍ مَّرِيجٍ

But they denied the truth when it came to them, so they are in a confused condition.

Allah ﷻ summarizes their spiritual state: they rejected the truth (al-haqq) when it came to them. And the result of this rejection is that they now exist in a state of (مَّرِيجٍ)—confusion, disorder, chaos.

The word marij is linguistically fascinating. It comes from the same root as the word for marriage (merging). In Arabic, words describing confusion often relate to things mixing together or fusing in a way that makes them indistinguishable. When two things merge and you can no longer tell them apart, confusion results. Conversely, clarity comes from separation—when things are distinct and separate, you can see them clearly.

By rejecting the truth, these disbelievers have lost the ability to distinguish truth from falsehood, right from wrong. Their rejection hasn’t led to certainty in their position; instead, it has led to internal chaos and confusion. They’re mixed up in their understanding of reality itself.

Ayah 6

أَفَلَمْ يَنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا وَزَيَّنَّـٰهَا وَمَا لَهَا مِن فُرُوجٍ

Have they not looked at the heaven above them—how We structured it and adorned it, and [how] it has no rifts?

Now begins a powerful sequence in which Allah ﷻ redirects their attention to the natural world around them—evidence that should shake them out of their denial. The question posed is simple yet profound: Haven’t they looked at the sky above them?

Allah ﷻ draws attention to three aspects of the heavens: First, how We built it (كَيْفَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا). The word bana means to construct or build, suggesting engineering and intentional design. Second, how We adorned it (وَزَيَّنَّـٰهَا)—We beautified it, decorated it with stars and celestial bodies. Third, it has no cracks (وَمَا لَهَا مِن فُرُوجٍ)—no rifts, no flaws, no inconsistencies in its construction.

When Allah ﷻ asks, “How did We build it?” He’s inviting reflection and study. This is an invitation to the astronomer, the physicist, the student of the cosmos. Investigate the heavens—study how they were constructed. The more you study, the more you’ll see the perfection and intentionality in creation.

Ayah 7

وَٱلْأَرْضَ مَدَدْنَـٰهَا وَأَلْقَيْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ

And the earth—We spread it out and cast therein firmly set mountains and made grow therein [something] of every joyous pair.

After directing attention upward to the sky, Allah ﷻ now directs it downward to the earth. He describes three divine actions: First, We spread it out (مَدَدْنَـٰهَا)—We extended it, made it vast and suitable for habitation. Second, We placed in it mountains (رَوَٰسِىَ)—firm, unmovable anchors. The word rawasi comes from rasa, meaning something firmly established that doesn’t shift. Third, We made grow in it pairs of every beautiful kind (مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ).

This is the domain of the geologist studying the earth’s structure and the mountains’ role in stabilizing the crust. This is the botanist’s domain, studying the incredible diversity of plant life—how plants grow in pairs, how they bring joy (bahij) through their colors, their fruits, their beauty. The word bahij suggests not just any plants, but joyous, delightful, beautiful vegetation that brings happiness to those who see it.

Ayah 8

تَبْصِرَةً وَذِكْرَىٰ لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ

As enlightenment and a reminder for every servant who turns [to Allah].

All of these signs in creation—the structured heavens, the adorned sky, the spread earth, the anchoring mountains, the beautiful plants—serve two purposes: tabsirah and dhikra.

(تَبْصِرَةً) means insight, enlightenment, something that makes you truly see. It comes from (sight), but it’s deeper than just seeing with the eyes—it’s seeing with understanding and perception. These signs give you baseerah, insight into the nature of reality.

(ذِكْرَىٰ) means reminder. These signs remind you of truths you already know deep down: that there is a Creator, that He is powerful, that He is wise, that He will resurrect you just as He created you the first time.

But notice the qualification: these signs serve as insight and reminder for every servant who turns back (لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ). The word muneeb comes from anaba, meaning to return or turn back. These signs benefit those who are willing to turn toward Allah ﷻ, those whose hearts are open to receiving guidance. The one who stubbornly turns away will look at the same sky, the same earth, the same mountains and plants, and see nothing but random material.

Ayah 9

وَنَزَّلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً مُّبَـٰرَكًا فَأَنۢبَتْنَا بِهِۦ جَنَّـٰتٍ وَحَبَّ ٱلْحَصِيدِ

And We have sent down from the sky blessed rain and made grow thereby gardens and grain that is harvested.

Allah ﷻ continues describing His signs in nature, now focusing on rain and what it produces. The rain is described as (مُّبَـٰرَكًا)—blessed, full of barakah.

What is barakah? The concept of barakah means bringing out hidden potential and goodness. Rain brings barakah to the earth because the earth contains seeds with potential—potential that cannot be realized without water. The seed sits dormant in the soil, full of possibility but unable to manifest that possibility on its own. When blessed rain comes, it awakens that potential, and suddenly gardens spring forth.

Through this rain, Allah ﷻ causes (جَنَّـٰتٍ)—gardens—to grow, and (حَبَّ ٱلْحَصِيدِ)—grain that is harvested. Notice the progression in imagery: first, gardens, which evoke fruit trees and the beauty of picking fresh fruit. Then habb al-hasid, which refers to grain crops like wheat and barley that grow tall and must be cut down and harvested. This covers both the fruits we pluck and the grains we harvest—the full spectrum of agricultural bounty.

Ayah 10

وَٱلنَّخْلَ بَاسِقَـٰتٍ لَّهَا طَلْعٌ نَّضِيدٌ

And palm trees soaring high, with layered spathes.

After mentioning gardens and grain in general, Allah ﷻ singles out the date palm for special mention. The palm tree is described as (بَاسِقَـٰتٍ)—soaring, towering high. Palm trees can grow incredibly tall, their trunks rising straight and strong toward the sky.

These palms have (طَلْعٌ نَّضِيدٌ)—clustered dates arranged in layers. The word tal’ refers to the emerging fruit clusters of the palm, and nadid means arranged in neat, organized layers or stacks. If you’ve ever seen how dates grow on a palm tree, you know they grow in large bunches, with the individual dates packed closely together in organized patterns. This is tal’un nadid—a beautiful description of the structured, abundant fruit of the date palm.

Ayah 11

رِّزْقًا لِّلْعِبَادِ ۖ وَأَحْيَيْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخُرُوجُ

As provision for the servants, and We give life by it to a dead land. Thus is the resurrection.

All of this—the gardens, the grain, the towering date palms—exists as (رِّزْقًا), as provision for the servants of Allah ﷻ. This word rizq encompasses all forms of sustenance that Allah ﷻ provides to maintain life.

Then comes the powerful parallel: We give life by it to a dead land (وَأَحْيَيْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا). The land was dead—barren, unable to produce anything, lifeless. Then the blessed rain came, and suddenly that same land burst forth with life, with greenery, with fruits and grains. What was dead is now alive.

And then the conclusion that ties everything together: Thus is the resurrection (كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخُرُوجُ). Just like that—just as easily as We bring dead land back to life—will be your coming forth from the graves. The word khuruj means coming out, emerging, exiting. Just as the plant emerges from the dead soil when rain comes, you will emerge from your graves when Allah ﷻ commands it. If you understand how rain revives dead earth, you should have no difficulty understanding how Allah ﷻ will revive dead bodies.

Ayah 12-14

كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلرَّسِّ وَثَمُودُ

وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ وَإِخْوَٰنُ لُوطٍ

وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ وَقَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ ۚ كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ

The people of Nuh denied before them, and the companions of the well, and Thamud, and ‘Ad, and Pharaoh, and the brothers of Lut, and the companions of the thicket, and the people of Tubba’. Each of them denied the messengers, so My threat was justified.

Now Allah ﷻ shifts to historical precedent, listing nations that rejected their messengers and faced the consequences. This rapid-fire list serves as both warning and proof: the Quraysh are not the first to deny revelation, and they won’t be the last to face consequences if they persist.

The people of Nuh (AS) denied for centuries until the flood came. (أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلرَّسِّ)—the companions of the well—refers to a people who threw their messenger into a deep well, attempting to silence the truth. The word rass means a deep well. Thamud carved homes in mountains and were destroyed. ‘Ad built towering structures and were destroyed by a fierce wind. Pharaoh claimed divinity and was drowned. The brothers of Lut (AS)—the people of Sodom—persisted in their moral corruption and were destroyed. (أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ)—the companions of the thicket—refers to the people of Madyan who lived among giant trees. The people of Tubba’ in Yemen also rejected and were destroyed.

The conclusion is clear: Each of them denied the messengers, so My threat was justified (كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ). The word (وَعِيدِ) means a threatening promise, a warning of punishment. When that warning is ignored long enough, it haqqah—it becomes justified, it comes to pass, it materializes into reality.

Ayah 15

أَفَعَيِينَا بِٱلْخَلْقِ ٱلْأَوَّلِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى لَبْسٍ مِّنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ

Did We fail in the first creation? But they are in confusion over a new creation.

After listing the destroyed nations, Allah ﷻ returns to the argument about resurrection. The question is rhetorical and powerful: Did We become tired or overwhelmed or fail in creating the universe and life the first time? Did creating the heavens and the earth, the mountains and the seas, human beings and all other creatures—did any of this exhaust Us or prove too difficult?

The obvious answer is no. If Allah ﷻ had the power to create everything from nothing the first time, why would recreating it be difficult? Why would resurrection be impossible for the One who originated creation?

But despite this obvious logic, they are in confusion about a new creation (بَلْ هُمْ فِى لَبْسٍ مِّنْ خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ). The word (لَبْسٍ) means confusion, but it literally comes from the root meaning “clothing.” Just as clothing covers and conceals the body, creating a mystery about what lies beneath, labs describes a state of mental covering—confusion that obscures truth. They’re confused about khalqin jadid—a new creation, the recreation of humanity on the Day of Resurrection.

Ayah 16

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ وَنَعْلَمُ مَا تُوَسْوِسُ بِهِۦ نَفْسُهُۥ ۖ وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ ٱلْوَرِيدِ

And We have already created man and know what his soul whispers to him, and We are closer to him than [his] jugular vein.

This ayah takes us into the profound reality of Allah’s ﷻ knowledge and closeness to the human being. Allah ﷻ declares: We created the human being, and We know what his nafs whispers to him.

The word (وَسْوَسَة) refers to whispers—but not just any whispers. These are the persistent, subtle whispers that come from within. We often associate waswasah with Shaytan’s whispers, but here Allah ﷻ tells us that the nafs itself engages in waswasah. The nafs—that aspect of ourselves connected to our desires, appetites, and impulses—constantly whispers to us. “You’re hungry.” “You’re tired.” “That looks attractive.” “You deserve this.” “Just this once.”

Understanding Islamic psychology is crucial here. The Quran describes multiple dimensions of the human personality: the qalb (heart), the aql (intellect—literally “that which restrains”), the ruh (spirit), and the nafs (the self, with its various states). The heart is the battlefield. The nafs pushes toward fulfilling desires and appetites. The ruh pulls toward spiritual elevation and connection with Allah ﷻ. The aql tries to balance and regulate.

Within the nafs itself, we find different states: an-nafs al-ammarah bis-su’ (the self that commands evil), an-nafs al-lawwamah (the self-reproaching self), and an-nafs al-mutma’innah (the self at peace). The journey of spiritual growth involves transforming the nafs from its lower states to its higher states.

Modern psychology, even without revelation, has recognized similar realities. Sigmund Freud, the father of modern psychology, developed a model of the human personality with three components: the Id (pure desires and appetites, similar to the untrained nafs), the Ego (the intellect that strategizes how to fulfill desires appropriately, similar to the aql), and the Super-Ego (which he described as seeking perfection, including spiritual goals, and which criticizes and prohibits drives and fantasies—remarkably similar to the concept of ruh).

While Freud reached wrong conclusions about the Super-Ego’s function due to his lack of revelation, his observations demonstrate that even through pure observation, humans can recognize that there’s something more to us than mere animal instinct—something spiritual, something seeking perfection.

Allah ﷻ knows every whisper of the nafs. Every fleeting thought, every temptation, every internal struggle—nothing is hidden from Him.

And then comes the statement that should transform how we understand Allah’s ﷻ presence: We are closer to him than his jugular vein (وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ ٱلْوَرِيدِ).

The jugular vein (habl al-wareed) is called wareed from the word meaning “place where water comes out.” It looks like a rope (habl) in the neck, and when cut, blood gushes out like water from a fountain. Allah ﷻ says He is closer to you than this vein—one of the most internal, intimate parts of your physical body.

What does this mean practically? It means Allah ﷻ is closer than we can possibly imagine. And this is where we must be careful not to make the mistake of trying to understand the unseen world through the limitations of the seen world. Questions like “Where is Allah ﷻ?” or “How can He be close to everyone?” apply dimensions of the physical world (above, below, inside, outside, here, there) to the One who exists beyond the physical world.

There are two realities: the world of the seen (’alam ash-shahadah) and the world of the unseen (’alam al-ghayb). Allah ﷻ exists in the unseen realm. We cannot comprehend His existence using the dimensional framework of the seen world. When Allah ﷻ says He is close, He is telling us a truth about our relationship with Him—a truth we accept and internalize, even if we cannot fully grasp it with our limited intellect. He is qareeb—near, close, accessible. That’s what matters for our hearts and our lives.

Ayah 17-18

إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى ٱلْمُتَلَقِّيَانِ عَنِ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ ٱلشِّمَالِ قَعِيدٌ

مَّا يَلْفِظُ مِن قَوْلٍ إِلَّا لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ

When the two receivers receive, seated on the right and on the left. He does not utter a word except that with him is an observer prepared [to record].

After mentioning the jugular vein—an image associated with death—Allah ﷻ transitions to describing what happens at the time of death and beyond. Two angels will appear, positioned on the right and on the left, (قَعِيدٌ)—seated, stationed, ready.

These are (ٱلْمُتَلَقِّيَانِ)—the two receivers. They receive what? They receive and record every deed, every word, every action of the human being throughout life.

The next ayah emphasizes the comprehensiveness of this recording: He does not utter a word (مَّا يَلْفِظُ مِن قَوْلٍ)—not a single utterance, not a single syllable—except that with him is an observer prepared (إِلَّا لَدَيْهِ رَقِيبٌ عَتِيدٌ).

The word (يَلْفِظُ) means to pronounce or articulate. It’s the same root we use in tajweed when we talk about makhraj (points of articulation)—al-lafẓ (pronunciation). Every single word that leaves your mouth is recorded.

The angel is described with two attributes: (رَقِيبٌ)—a watcher, an observer, someone constantly monitoring—and (عَتِيدٌ)—ready, prepared, present, and equipped to record immediately. Nothing slips by. No word is too insignificant to be recorded. Every careless joke, every harsh word, every beautiful supplication, every word of comfort—all of it is being recorded by angels who are ready and present at all times.

Ayah 19

وَجَآءَتْ سَكْرَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنتَ مِنْهُ تَحِيدُ

And the intoxication of death will bring the truth; that is what you were trying to avoid.

Now comes the moment of death itself. Allah ﷻ describes it as (سَكْرَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ)—the intoxication of death, the overwhelming stupor of death. The word sakrah comes from the same root as sukr (drunkenness). Just as intoxication causes a person to drift in and out of consciousness, losing control of their faculties, death brings a similar state—a fading in and out, a loss of connection with the physical world.

This intoxication of death comes with the truth (بِٱلْحَقِّ). At the moment of death, all pretenses fall away. The truth becomes undeniable. The reality you spent your life denying or ignoring crashes over you with unavoidable force.

And Allah ﷻ addresses the one facing death: That is what you were trying to avoid (ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنتَ مِنْهُ تَحِيدُ). The word (تَحِيدُ) means to avoid, to run away from, to flee from in fear. Throughout life, you ran from this reality. You avoided thinking about death. You were terrified of confronting the truth of what comes after. But now it has arrived, and there is no more running.

Ayah 20

وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْوَعِيدِ

And the Horn will be blown. That is the Day of the Warning.

The scene shifts from individual death to collective resurrection. The (ٱلصُّورِ)—the trumpet or horn—will be blown, and this marks the Day of the Warning (يَوْمُ ٱلْوَعِيدِ).

Each person dies at their own time—scattered across centuries and millennia. But the resurrection is collective. When the horn is blown, all of humanity will be raised together, regardless of when they died. The person who died a thousand years ago and the person who died yesterday will stand together on the same plain.

This is yawm al-wa’id—the Day when all the warnings come to fulfillment, when all the threatening promises materialize, when everything the prophets warned about becomes reality.

Ayah 21

وَجَآءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّعَهَا سَآئِقٌ وَشَهِيدٌ

And every soul will come, with it a driver and a witness.

On that Day, every single soul (kul nafs)—every person who ever lived—will arrive with two angels accompanying them. One is a (سَآئِقٌ)—a driver, one who herds or pushes forward. The other is a (شَهِيدٌ)—a witness.

The image of the sa’iq is powerful. Think of a prisoner being escorted by guards, being hurried along: “Move it, keep walking, no stopping.” For the one who lived as a criminal against Allah’s ﷻ law, the angel will drive them forward roughly toward their reckoning. But this same word can also evoke a VIP being escorted through a crowd by security, being protected and guided safely to their destination. For the righteous, the angels will guide them with honor and care.

The shaheed is the witness—the angel who will testify about this person’s life. This angel has the complete record and will read it out. No one can say, “I didn’t do that,” or “That’s not accurate,” because the witness was there for everything.

Ayah 22

لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِى غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَآءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ

[It will be said], “You were certainly in unmindfulness of this, and We have removed from you your cover, so your sight today is sharp.”

The final ayah of this passage contains a devastating statement addressed to the one who denied and ignored the warnings throughout their life. They will be told: You were in heedlessness of this (لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِى غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا).

The word (غَفْلَةٍ) means heedlessness, neglect, unmindfulness, oblivion. You were living in a state of complete unawareness. You didn’t pay attention. You were distracted by the dunya. You ignored every sign, every warning, every reminder. You lived as though this Day would never come.

But We have removed from you your cover (فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَآءَكَ). The word ghiṭa’ (غِطَآء) means a cover or veil. This teaches us that in the worldly life, there is a veil over our eyes that prevents us from seeing the unseen reality. We can’t see the angels. We can’t see Allah ﷻ. We can’t see Jannah or Jahannam. We can’t see the full spiritual reality that surrounds us. This is by divine wisdom—we’re being tested based on belief in the unseen (al-ghayb).

But on the Day of Judgment, that veil will be ripped away. Suddenly, everything becomes visible. The hadith literature describes that the veil will be lifted and the believers will see Allah ﷻ—a vision that will be the greatest blessing of Jannah.

So your sight today is sharp (فَبَصَرُكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ). The word (حَدِيدٌ) means iron or something ironclad, sharp, piercing, unflinching. Your vision today is crystal clear. Everything you doubted, everything you denied, everything you ignored—it’s all right in front of you now with absolute clarity. No more confusion. No more labs or marij. Just pure, undeniable, overwhelming truth.

This is the warning and the reminder contained in these opening passages of Surah Qaf: death is coming, resurrection is real, and on that Day, there will be no more room for denial.

Ayah 23–45 · linked video: 02. Qaf (Ayah 23-45)

Surah Qaf: Ayahs 23-45

Ayah 23

وَقَالَ قَرِينُهُۥ هَـٰذَا مَا لَدَىَّ عَتِيدٌ

His companion, his قَرِين , presents himself before Allah ﷻ. This qarin is the shaytan that was assigned to accompany this person throughout their life, constantly working to lead them astray. The shaytan approaches as though he had been employed by Allah ﷻ for a specific task, saying: “This is the one that’s been presented before You. This is the one I’ve got ready.” The word عَتِيد carries the meaning of something prepared and ready to be presented.

Ayah 24

أَلْقِيَا فِى جَهَنَّمَ كُلَّ كَفَّارٍ عَنِيدٍ

Allah ﷻ issues the command in the dual form: throw into Jahannam every كَفَّار and عَنِيد . The word kaffar describes one who was extremely ungrateful, persistently denying and rejecting the truth. Aneed refers to someone who was extremely stubborn and obstinate in their rejection—شَدِيد الْعِنَاد , displaying severe stubbornness and resistance to guidance.

Ayah 25

مَّنَّاعٍ لِّلْخَيْرِ مُعْتَدٍ مُّرِيبٍ

The description continues: مَّنَّاعٍ لِّلْخَيْرِ —the kind of person who prevented all good. This has two dimensions to understand. First, it describes how the qarin, the shaytan companion, constantly tried to stop the person from doing good. Imagine you have 53 minutes before Salah. You could sit and make dhikr, engage in tasbih, but the shaytan comes whispering: “I heard there’s a new game that just came out on your phone. Why don’t you download it and play?” You listen to this suggestion, download the game, and then the moment you get up from that, it’s time for Salah. But the shaytan isn’t finished: “Wait, wait. You have plenty of time for Salah. Relax, take a nap.” You take that nap, and when you wake up, the time for Salah has passed. You think, “I should make it up,” but again comes the whisper: “Make it up later. You have other things to do right now.” The manna’in lil-khayr keeps preventing you from doing good, one excuse after another.

The second dimension is more sobering: the person who listens to the shaytan constantly eventually becomes someone who prevents others from doing good. They become the reason good doesn’t happen in their sphere of influence. Consider the Muslim households where a young man wants to learn something about the Quran, or a wife wants to wear hijab, but the husband forbids it: “No, you’re not going to do that here.” She wants to establish her prayers regularly, but he says, “Not in this house. That’s not going to happen. I can’t allow it.” There are sisters who cry their hearts out, saying, “I want to send my child to Islamic school, but my husband says no—absolutely not, it’s not going to happen.”

مُعْتَدٍ —one who crosses the line, transgressing boundaries that Allah ﷻ has set. مُّرِيبٍ —always in doubt themselves and putting others in doubt, spreading skepticism and uncertainty about the truth.

Ayah 26

ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَأَلْقِيَاهُ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلشَّدِيدِ

The one who placed another god besides Allah ﷻ. We understand from this revelation who that other god was: himself. He made his own desires, his own nafs, into an object of worship. The command is repeated: throw him into the intense, severe punishment.

Ayah 27

۞ قَالَ قَرِينُهُۥ رَبَّنَا مَآ أَطْغَيْتُهُۥ وَلَـٰكِن كَانَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ

Now the qarin, the shaytan companion who is also about to be thrown into the Fire, protests. He says: “Our Master, I didn’t make him rebel! He himself was in ضَلَـٰل بَعِيد —he was way out there in misguidance. He was far gone on his own. I didn’t have to work that hard. It’s not my fault.” The shaytan attempts to deflect responsibility, claiming the person was already predisposed to deviation.

Ayah 28

قَالَ لَا تَخْتَصِمُوا۟ لَدَىَّ وَقَدْ قَدَّمْتُ إِلَيْكُم بِٱلْوَعِيدِ

Allah ﷻ responds: “Don’t argue before Me. Don’t give Me debates.” The matter is settled. وَقَدْ قَدَّمْتُ إِلَيْكُم بِٱلْوَعِيدِ—the strongest possible threat, the clearest warning, had already been issued to you beforehand. You were warned in the world. The message reached you. The consequences were made clear.

Ayah 29

مَا يُبَدَّلُ ٱلْقَوْلُ لَدَىَّ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ

The verdict will not be changed before Me today. What has been decreed is final. وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ—and I am not the One doing any wrong to My slaves. No injustice is being committed here. Every person receives exactly what their choices in the world earned them.

Ayah 30

يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ ٱمْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِن مَّزِيدٍ

The scene intensifies as we witness people being sent into Jahannam one after another after another—a continuous stream of those who rejected faith and persisted in wrongdoing. Then Allah ﷻ turns to Jahannam itself and asks: هَلِ ٱمْتَلَأْتِ —“Are you full? Have you eaten enough human beings and jinn? Have you had enough to consume?”

The response is chilling: هَلْ مِن مَّزِيدٍ —“Is there any more? Can I have some more, please?” Despite being so filled that earlier those already in it cried out that there was no more room, that they didn’t want more people coming in because there was no space left, Jahannam itself expresses an insatiable hunger. It wants to consume more.

Ayah 31

وَأُزْلِفَتِ ٱلْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ

In complete contrast to the horrifying scene of Jahannam, Jannah is brought near. وَأُزْلِفَتِ means it is lowered and brought close, brought down and positioned near for the people of taqwa, those who were conscious of Allah ﷻ in their worldly lives. غَيْرَ بَعِيدٍ—it’s not far at all. Even though believers in the world were being told, “Don’t worry, it’s coming, it’s coming,” they’re now being reassured that they haven’t been made to wait long. From their perspective, it’s right there.

Ayah 32

هَـٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ

This is what you all have been promised. But it’s not for everyone—it’s لِكُلِّ أَوَّابٍ حَفِيظٍ. Awwab describes the one who keeps coming back to Allah ﷻ, who constantly returns in repentance. No matter how many times they stumble, they turn back to their Lord.

Hafeez—the one who guarded. When we examine the word حَفِيظ in the Quran, we find Allah ﷻ talks about guarding our shame and modesty: الَّذِينَ هُمْ لِفُرُوجِهِمْ حَافِظُونَ (those who guard their private parts). Allah ﷻ also speaks of guarding the amanah, the trust. We learn that Allah ﷻ offered this trust to the heavens and the earth, but they refused to carry it because they couldn’t bear its weight. Yet the human being carried it—that responsibility of choice, that burden of moral agency. The revelation we received, the honor of free will that Allah ﷻ bestowed upon us, the ability to choose between right and wrong—the hafeez guarded that choice. He guarded that honor and took that responsibility seriously.

Ayah 33

مَّنْ خَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَجَآءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُّنِيبٍ

The description continues: مَّنْ خَشِىَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنَ بِٱلْغَيْبِ —the one who feared Ar-Rahman in the unseen. The mercy and love of Allah ﷻ exist in realms we cannot fully perceive. Sometimes we don’t realize in the moment how He is being merciful to us, how His wisdom operates through what seems difficult. But this person believed in that unseen mercy. Even more, when nobody else was around to see, when there were no witnesses to their actions, they would still maintain their consciousness of Ar-Rahman.

وَجَآءَ بِقَلْبٍ مُّنِيبٍ —and they came before Allah ﷻ with a heart that’s turning back to Him, a heart in a state of sincere repentance and devotion.

Ayah 34

ٱدْخُلُوهَا بِسَلَـٰمٍ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُلُودِ

They’re told: Enter it in peace, in safety, in serenity. ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُلُودِ—this is the Day of permanence, the Day of eternity. You will stay here forever. Unlike every good thing in the worldly life that eventually ended or was taken away, this blessing is eternal.

Ayah 35

لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ

They will have whatever they want. Whatever order they place, whatever desire enters their hearts, whatever they can possibly think of—they can have it. وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ —and We have something more. Beyond what they can imagine, beyond what they can conceive or articulate, Allah ﷻ has prepared additional blessings. They can have whatever their hearts desire, and on top of that, there’s something extra that surpasses their imagination.

Notice the deliberate contrast: when Jahannam was asked if it was full, it responded “hal min mazeed”—is there more to consume? But for the people of Jannah, Allah ﷻ uses the same word mazeed in the opposite sense—there is always more goodness, more blessing, more beauty to be granted to them.

Ayah 36

وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَشَدُّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا فَنَقَّبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ هَلْ مِن مَّحِيصٍ

The discourse shifts dramatically. How many nations, how many generations We destroyed before them—nations that were أَشَدُّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا , more powerful than the Quraysh in terms of their ability to grab, squeeze, punish, and seize. Their grip on power was far stronger.

فَنَقَّبُوا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ —this phrase has been understood in two complementary ways, and perhaps both meanings are intended. Naqqaba means to go out and around in the land. The first interpretation: We gave these nations tremendous power, and they used it to seize other lands. They went around conquering territories, expanding vast empires, building huge civilizations. They seemed invincible in their time.

The second interpretation: when Allah ﷻ destroyed them, when His punishment came upon them, they were running around desperately in every country they could reach, fleeing in terror, trying to escape death.

هَلْ مِن مَّحِيصٍ —is there any place they can run now? Those same powerful civilizations that conquered territories without difficulty—do they have anywhere to escape? Can they protect themselves from death? The answer, of course, is no. No power, no empire, no amount of conquest could save them from the decree of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 37

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَذِكْرَىٰ لِمَن كَانَ لَهُۥ قَلْبٌ أَوْ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّمْعَ وَهُوَ شَهِيدٌ

No doubt, in all of that—in these accounts of destroyed nations, in the realities of the Hereafter, in the scenes of judgment—there is a powerful reminder لِمَن كَانَ لَهُۥ قَلْبٌ , for the person who has a heart. These are people with القَلْب السَّلِيم , a sound heart that’s already been mentioned in the surah. When such people hear this revelation, it appeals to them immediately. The idea that there’s a life after this one makes perfect sense. It’s an easy transition for them to accept and internalize—like the example of Abu Bakr (RA), for whom belief came naturally and easily.

But then Allah ﷻ mentions another type: أَوْ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّمْعَ وَهُوَ شَهِيدٌ —or there’s a person who lends their ear. This person may not have been particularly spiritual before encountering Islam, but they say, “Okay, let me at least hear what you have to say.” They listen carefully. They drop their preconceptions and pay genuine attention. They become witnesses to what they’re hearing—meaning they’re present mentally and emotionally, not just physically hearing sounds. This is the intellectual type, the one who approaches through reason and evidence.

So Allah ﷻ presents two paths to guidance: the spiritual heart that recognizes truth immediately, and the attentive mind that investigates and concludes rationally. Both are valid, both are honored.

Ayah 38

وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ وَمَا مَسَّنَا مِن لُّغُوبٍ

We already created the heavens and the earth and whatever exists between them in six days. وَمَا مَسَّنَا مِن لُّغُوبٍ —and no exhaustion, no weariness, no sense of being overburdened touched Us whatsoever.

This ayah responds to a concept found in earlier scriptures that had been corrupted, where it was claimed that on the seventh day, God rested and established the Sabbath as a day of rest. The implication was that the Creator needed to recover from the exertion of creation. Allah ﷻ categorically denies this: We didn’t get exhausted at all. He is above any limitation, any weakness, any need for rest or recuperation.

Ayah 39

فَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَقَبْلَ ٱلْغُرُوبِ

Be patient over what they’re saying—over their denials, their mockery, their rejection. And declare the perfection of your Master, glorifying Him. قَبْلَ طُلُوعِ ٱلشَّمْسِ —before the sun rises. وَقَبْلَ ٱلْغُرُوبِ —and before it sets.

This ayah was revealed before the five daily prayers were fully established and made obligatory. The Prophet ﷺ was being instructed that there are special times when declaring Allah’s perfection and engaging in worship is particularly important: the time before sunrise and the time before sunset.

Ayah 40

وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَأَدْبَـٰرَ ٱلسُّجُودِ

And during a portion of the night, declare His perfection. وَأَدْبَـٰرَ ٱلسُّجُودِ —and at the conclusion of sujud, at the heels of prostration. Among the three major components of Salah—standing (qiyam), bowing (ruku’), and prostration (sujud)—the last and most significant portion is the sujud. So this phrase means: when you’re done with the sujud, when you’ve completed the prayer, engage in special remembrance of Allah ﷻ.

This is where the practice of dhikr after Salah originates. The Prophet ﷺ taught us precisely how to implement this command: after completing the prayer, we say SubhanAllah 33 times, Alhamdulillah 33 times, and Allahu Akbar 33 times, along with other prescribed remembrances. This entire practice that the Prophet ﷺ established is inspired by this ayah—at the conclusion of every sujud, at the end of every prayer, we engage in tasbih.

Ayah 41

وَٱسْتَمِعْ يَوْمَ يُنَادِ ٱلْمُنَادِ مِن مَّكَانٍ قَرِيبٍ

And listen carefully—the Day when the caller will call from a nearby place. The command to the Prophet ﷺ to “listen” indicates something tremendously significant is coming. Some scholars have explained that this references the special call that will summon the Prophet ﷺ to his المَقَام المَحْمُود , his praised station on the Day of Judgment. The caller will be nearby, making it a convenience specifically for the Prophet ﷺ, honoring his elevated rank.

Ayah 42

يَوْمَ يَسْمَعُونَ ٱلصَّيْحَةَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُرُوجِ

The Day when they will hear the Blast—الصَّيْحَة , the loud, piercing scream—بِٱلْحَقِّ , with truth, with purpose, with reality. There’s no escaping it, no denying it. ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلْخُرُوجِ —that is the Day of coming out, the Day of emergence from the graves, the Day of resurrection.

Ayah 43

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نُحْىِۦ وَنُمِيتُ وَإِلَيْنَا ٱلْمَصِيرُ

No doubt, We alone are the ones who give life and We are the ones who give death. وَإِلَيْنَا ٱلْمَصِيرُ —and to Us alone is the final return. Everything comes back to Allah ﷻ. Every soul will stand before Him.

Ayah 44

يَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ ٱلْأَرْضُ عَنْهُمْ سِرَاعًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ حَشْرٌ عَلَيْنَا يَسِيرٌ

The Day when the earth will crack open, splitting apart. سِرَاعًا —and they will emerge rushing, racing out quickly. All you’ll see on that Day is intense rushing—the earth cracking and shifting, and people running everywhere in a state of urgency and confusion.

ذَٰلِكَ حَشْرٌ عَلَيْنَا يَسِيرٌ —that gathering, that resurrection, is easy for Us. It’s not going to be difficult for Us to accomplish at all. The same Power that created everything from nothing can certainly reassemble what once existed.

Ayah 45

نَّحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَقُولُونَ ۖ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِجَبَّارٍ ۖ فَذَكِّرْ بِٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَن يَخَافُ وَعِيدِ

We know very well what they’re saying—all their objections, their mockery, their arguments and denials. Nothing they say is hidden from Us.

وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِجَبَّارٍ —and you are not a tyrant over them. You’re not going to bully them. You cannot and should not force them to accept this message. You don’t have that kind of compulsive authority over their hearts.

فَذَكِّرْ بِٱلْقُرْءَانِ مَن يَخَافُ وَعِيدِ —so just remind with the Quran whoever truly fears My warning.

This final ayah is profoundly inspirational for anyone who wants to share the message of the Quran with the world, with their families, with their friends. Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ—and through him, tells all of us: We know what the disbelievers say. We hear their rejection. But remember, you can’t force anybody to change. You don’t even know with certainty who will be receptive and who won’t, who truly fears Allah’s warning and who doesn’t. Anyone might accept the guidance at any moment.

All you can do—and all you’re required to do—is remind people with the Quran. Keep sharing the message. Keep presenting the truth. The guidance of hearts belongs to Allah ﷻ alone, but the duty of conveying the reminder belongs to us. And that is enough.
